one_line_takeaway: SWhisper shows that jailbreak prompts can be covertly delivered to speech-driven LLMs via inaudible near-ultrasound audio on ordinary consumer speakers/microphones, using channel-inversion compensation plus a voice-friendly, phonetically-constrained adversarial suffix, achieving up to 0.94 NR/0.93 SC on commercial models with jailbreak audio statistically indistinguishable from background noise to human listeners (32-participant study, all p>=0.523).
key_finding: SWhisper achieves the best NR/SC among tested methods on most targets, e.g. up to 0.940 NR / 0.925 SC on GLM-4-Air and 0.780/0.740 on Grok-4 (white-box on Llama-3.1-8B: 0.960/0.945). On end-to-end LALMs it substantially outperforms baselines, e.g. on GLM-4-Voice it reaches 0.980 NR / 0.813 SC, beating the strongest baseline (Auto-DAN) by 0.240 NR and 0.270 SC. Its generated prompts achieve the lowest WER among all evaluated jailbreak methods (GCG/IGCG WER often >0.75, sometimes >1.0; PAIR/Auto-DAN <0.05), explaining its superior speech-channel transfer. Under diverse real-world conditions (noise 50-60dB across office/park/restaurant/street, distance 1-4m, angle 0-90deg, three recording devices) performance stays largely stable, e.g. NR only drops from 0.780 (1m) to 0.760 (4m) and stays at 0.700 NR/0.690 SC even at 90deg off-axis. Ablations show removing the structured prompt template causes the largest degradation (NR 0.780->0.178, SC 0.733->0.169), more than removing the optimized suffix (NR->0.740, SC->0.703) or semantic regularization (NR->0.660, SC->0.645). The user study found no statistically significant human ability to distinguish jailbreak audio from background-only playback in any tested environment (all p>=0.523; average ABX accuracy 0.475-0.525, near chance; mean Likert 'indistinguishable' scores >=4.92/5). One notable failure case: on DeepSeek, the strongest baseline (Auto-DAN, Gemma-3-4B surrogate) slightly exceeds SWhisper's best result (0.820/0.820 vs 0.780/0.745).
core_contribution: SWhisper is the first end-to-end framework for inaudible, black-box jailbreak prompt delivery against speech-driven LLMs using only commodity speakers and microphones. It combines (1) a general-purpose near-ultrasound covert acoustic channel that encodes arbitrary baseband audio (e.g., a full spoken jailbreak prompt) into 17-22 kHz near-ultrasonic waveforms via empirical modeling of the microphone's nonlinear transfer function and regularized channel-inversion pre-compensation, with (2) a voice-friendly jailbreak prompt construction method that combines structured instruction templates with a suffix optimized under a semantic-regularization loss, a phonetically-constrained vocabulary, and a parallel/simulated-annealing token update scheme, plus simulated channel distortions during optimization for robustness. The authors frame jailbreaking as a case study demonstrating a broader covert prompt-injection/command-execution channel.
threat_model: Black-box attacker with no access to the victim device or user and no white-box access to the target model (only observable audio input/output behavior); the attacker does have white-box access to a surrogate model for offline suffix optimization to enable transfer. The attack must succeed in a single audio query (no multi-turn interaction). Hardware is restricted to commodity/consumer-grade speakers and microphones (e.g., laptop or smart speaker for playback, phone/commercial mic for capture) -- no specialized ultrasonic transmitters. The injected audio is delivered via nearby devices or benign media and must remain imperceptible to humans while still demodulating correctly through microphone front-end nonlinearity. The goal is covert prompt injection, instantiated in this paper as jailbreaking (bypassing model safety alignment) but stated by the authors to generalize to other prompt-based/command-injection attacks.
limitations: The authors' own discussion notes: (1) channel modeling uses a single averaged transfer function across devices/environments rather than adaptive/environment-aware modeling, which could limit reliability under highly dynamic acoustic conditions; (2) the prompt template is structured but universal/non-task-specific, leaving room for context-aware template designs; (3) suffix optimization is not explicitly transfer-aware (no multi-model joint optimization objective), which the authors flag as a direction to further strengthen black-box effectiveness; (4) existing signal-based countermeasures (ultrasonic feature tracking, correlation-based detection) were found unreliable on commodity devices in the authors' own tests -- e.g. two of three tested recording devices (HIKVISION, iPhone 14 Pro) apply aggressive front-end low-pass filtering that removes near-ultrasonic energy and precludes correlation-based detection, while only Redmi Note 12 preserves it; (5) DeepSeek was the one target where a baseline (Auto-DAN) outperformed SWhisper. The paper does not report a defense/mitigation the authors validate as effective; it only surveys existing countermeasures and finds them insufficient.

datasets_used: AdvBench harmful-behavior subset (520 prompts; 50-prompt curated evaluation subset per standard protocol)
Freesound ambient-noise interference samples (office, park, restaurant, street)
quantitative_results: Text-based targets, best NR/SC: GLM-4-Air 0.940/0.925 (Qwen2.5-7B surrogate); Grok-4 0.780/0.740 (Mistral-7B surrogate); white-box Llama-3.1-8B 0.960/0.945
End-to-end LALM GLM-4-Voice: 0.980 NR / 0.813 SC, beating Auto-DAN baseline by +0.240 NR / +0.270 SC
End-to-end LALM Qwen-Omni-Turbo: 0.580 NR / 0.503 SC vs Auto-DAN 0.520/0.475
WER: SWhisper lowest among tested methods; GCG/IGCG WER often >0.75 (sometimes >1.0); PAIR/Auto-DAN <0.05
Robustness across noise scenarios (50-60dB, office/park/restaurant/street): NR consistently >0.720, SC >0.693; peak 0.780/0.723 at 50dB office/park
Angle robustness: 0deg NR 0.780/SC 0.733 down to 90deg NR 0.700/SC 0.690
Recording device robustness: HIKVISION 0.780/0.715, Redmi Note 12 0.680/0.635, iPhone 14 Pro 0.780/0.733
Ablation (Grok-4 target, Llama-3.1-8B surrogate): full SWhisper 0.780 NR/0.733 SC; w/o suffix 0.740/0.703; w/o structured template 0.178/0.169 (largest drop); w/o semantic regularization 0.660/0.645
User study (32 participants, 5 environments): ABX accuracy 0.475-0.525 (chance=0.5), p-values 0.516-0.829 (all >=0.523, not significant); Likert 'indistinguishable' mean scores 4.920-4.995 out of 5
DeepSeek exception: strongest baseline Auto-DAN (0.820/0.820) exceeded SWhisper's best (0.780/0.745)
